coredata.dc:title: A nanocomposite probe of graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles embedded in a selective polymer for the enrichment and detection of ceftazidime
coredata.dc:creator: Bunkoed, Opas
Raksawong, Phannika
Chaowana, Ratchada
Nurerk, Piyaluk
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles were embedded in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP) to develop a magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe that could enrich and detect trace ceftazidime in milk in conjunction with an optosensor. Graphene quantum dots enhanced the sensitivity of the optosensor and the specificity of the molecularly imprinted polymer reinforced the selectivity of the nanocomposite probe. The incorporated magnetite nanoparticles increased enrichment of the target analyte so that a smaller volume of detecting solution could be used. The developed probe was characterized and the preparation procedure and detection conditions were optimized. In the optimum conditions, linearity was in the range of 0.10–10.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.05 μg L−1. The developed system was utilized to detect ceftazidime in milk samples. Recoveries were in the range of 90.7–99.2% with RSD below 6% and the obtained results agreed well those obtained with chromatographic technique. However, the developed optosensor exhibited better sensitivity, was faster and easier to operate and can be modified for the enrichment and detection of other target analytes in various sample matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Magnetite nanoparticles
Ceftazidime
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots, magnetite nanoparticles composited with molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP)
Determination of ceftazidime by HPLC technique
Characterization of GQDs and nanocomposite fluorescence probe
Optimization of the synthesis of magnetic nanocomposite probe and detection conditions
Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
Effect of incubation time and stirring rate
Effect of amount of magnetite nanoparticles
Comparison of optosensor using nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe with other works

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of the GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe for ceftazidime detection.

TEM image of GQD nanoparticles (A), SEM image of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes (B), size distribution histogram of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe (C) and VSM curve of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe (D).
Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker for the fabrication of magnetic nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe.

Effect of incubation time of magnetic nanocomposite probe and target analyte and stirring rate (A): Effect of amount of Fe3O4@SiO2 (B), sample volume (C) and pH of nanocomposite probe solution (D) on sensitivity toward ceftazidime detection.

The fluorescence spectra in the presence of 0.0–10.0 μg L−1 ceftazidime of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP (A) and GQDs@Fe3O4/NIP (B) probes, calibration curves of the magnetic nanocomposite probe (C) and a photograph of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes under UV light with (right) and without (left) ceftazidime (D).
The selectivity of GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP nanoprobe (A) and competitive binding study (B).

The correlation between an optosensor using nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes and HPLC (A), and HPLC chromatograms of ceftazidime in spiked milk samples at 10.0 (a), 20.0 (b), 50.0 (c) and 100.0 (d) μg L−1 (B).
The determination of ceftazidime in milk samples using the developed optosensor and recoveries in spiked samples (n = 5).
The analytical performances of an optosensor using nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes and results for ceftazidime detection from previous works.

head.ce:title: A nanocomposite probe of graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles embedded in a selective polymer for the enrichment and detection of ceftazidime

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla, 90112, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla, 90112, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles were embedded in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP) to develop a magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe that could enrich and detect trace ceftazidime in milk in conjunction with an optosensor. Graphene quantum dots enhanced the sensitivity of the optosensor and the specificity of the molecularly imprinted polymer reinforced the selectivity of the nanocomposite probe. The incorporated magnetite nanoparticles increased enrichment of the target analyte so that a smaller volume of detecting solution could be used. The developed probe was characterized and the preparation procedure and detection conditions were optimized. In the optimum conditions, linearity was in the range of 0.10–10.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.05 μg L−1. The developed system was utilized to detect ceftazidime in milk samples. Recoveries were in the range of 90.7–99.2% with RSD below 6% and the obtained results agreed well those obtained with chromatographic technique. However, the developed optosensor exhibited better sensitivity, was faster and easier to operate and can be modified for the enrichment and detection of other target analytes in various sample matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Ceftazidime is a group of cephalosporin antibiotic with broad spectrum activity against Gram positive and negative bacteria [1]. It is normally administered in the treatment of bacterial infections and, in combination with other antibiotics. Although ceftazidime is generally well tolerated, side effects can include seizures, bloody diarrhea and hyperactivity [2]. Thus, the detection of ceftazidime in food is an important safeguard.

Ceftazidime in various samples has been detected with several analytical techniques. These techniques have included electrochemical detection [3,4], liquid chromatography [5,6], spectrophotometry [7,8] and spectrofluorimetry [9]. Fast detection, easy operation and inexpensive apparatus make spectrofluorimetry an interesting method of detecting ceftazidime and the sensitivity of the technique can be improved by the use of a fluorescent probe.

Developers of fluorescent nanoprobes for optosensor applications have turned toward the use of quantum dots (QDs). QDs exhibit unique optical properties such as size-dependent tunable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, high fluorescence intensity, wide excitation range and good photochemical stability [10,11]. QDs have been used in highly sensitive fluorescent probes for the detection of salicylic acid [12], copper ion [13] and organophosphorus [14]. Sensing applications have adopted several types of QDs as fluorescent probes. Cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs), cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs), carbon nanodots (CDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have all been used [11]. GQDs provided good water dispersibility, biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, high photostability and a high surface-to-volume ratio [15]. These characteristics of GQDs would be useful in an optical sensing system.

To improve the selectivity of an optosensor, a fluorescent probe can be composited with high specific affinity materials such as molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [16]. MIPs are greatly selective polymers, which can be prepared by copolymerization process in the presence of a template molecule, cross-linker and functional monomer. Removal of the template molecule leaves behind specific binding sites that complement the target analyte in size, shape and functional groups. In addition, MIPs are chemically stable, cost effective and easily synthesized under mild conditions. MIPs were applied as a specific material in solid phase extraction sorbents [17,18], solid phase microextraction [19,20], and electrode modification for electrochemical sensors [21,22]. A composite probe, such as the proposed device, can enrich or preconcentrate target analytes so that they can be detected at trace levels. The incorporation of magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) into the probe is an interesting modification normally used in the preparation of magnetic solid phase extraction sorbents to preconcentrate analytes such as cephalosporins [23], phthalates [24], sulfonamides [25] and benzophenones [26].

In this work, a sensitive and selective nanoprobe of GQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles embedded in MIP was developed and utilized for the enrichment and detection of ceftazidime. The synthesized nanocomposite probes were investigated and characterized and the detection system was optimized. The developed magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe was used to detect trace ceftazidime in milk and the accuracy of its results was compared with the results obtained from a HPLC technique.

Ceftazidime, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 96.0%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98.0%) and citric acid (98.0%) were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., Japan. FeCl2.4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O were from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) was purchased from Ajax finechem, Australia. Ammonium solution (NH4OH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets and acetonitrile were from RCI Labscan, Thailand.

Fluorescence was measured using an RF-5310 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra were produced with an Avaspec 2048 spectrometer (Avantes, Netherlands). The morphology of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEM-5200, JEOL, Japan). Surface area analysis (ASAP 2460 Micromeristic, USA) followed the Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method to determine the surface area of the fabricated magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized following a previous work with minor modification [25]. Briefly, 1.5 g of FeCl2·4H2O and 4.5 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and heated to 85 οC. Then, 10 mL of NH4OH (30% v/v) were added into the mixture solution, which was maintained at 80 οC for 1.0 h under vigorous stirring. After the resultant suspension had cooled down to room temperature (28 ± 2 °C), the solid phase was isolated using a magnet and washed with 20 mL of deionized water. The synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dried at 65 οC for 5 h. The surfaces of the dried Fe3O4 nanoparticles were then modified via a sol-gel process to synthesize Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. Briefly, 2.0 g of Fe3O4 was dispersed in a solution containing 2.5 mL of NH4OH (30% v/v), 150 mL of ethanol and 100 mL of deionized water. After sonication for 20 min, 2.0 mL of TEOS were added into the solution and stirred for 12 h at a constant 40 οC. The resultant black powder of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles was collected using a magnet, washed with 60 mL of deionized water and dried at 70 οC for 8 h.

GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid [27]. First, 2.0 g of citric acid were added to a 50 mL round bottom flask and, using the heating mantle, raised to 350 οC at which point the citric acid became an orange liquid. Separately, 1.5 mL of APTES were mixed with 100 mL of 0.25 M NaOH and stirred at 40 οC. The orange homogeneous liquid citric acid was added dropwise into the mixture solution of APTES and NaOH and stirred for 30 min. The obtained mixture solution was placed in a 12 kDa cut-off dialysis bag for 12 h to remove unreacted components. The obtained silica-embedded graphene quantum dots were stored as-prepared at 4 οC until used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots, magnetite nanoparticles composited with molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP)
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was synthesized by modifying a sol-gel polymerization reaction from a previous work [28]. 50 mg of the template molecule ceftazidime, 5.0 mL of ethanol and 2.0 mL of the functional monomer APTES were put into a brown bottle and stirred for 30 min. After 30 min, 5.0 mL of GQDs, 1.0 mL of the cross-linker TEOS, 5.0 mL of the catalyst NH4OH and 10 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 microparticles were added and the mixture was stirred for 4 h. To remove the template molecule, the obtained nanocomposite probes were washed with 10.0 mL ethanol (three times) and centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 15 min. The synthesized nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes were dried at 60 οC. A non-imprinted polymer nanocomposite (GQDs@Fe3O4/NIP) probe was also synthesized using the same procedure without the addition of the ceftazidime template molecule. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe for ceftazidime detection.

Magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probes were dispersed in 4.0 mL of sample solution and stirred for 20 min at 500 rpm. After complete adsorption, the probes were separated using an external magnet. To detect the analyte of interest, the separated nanocomposite fluorescent probes were dispersed in 150 μL of 10 mM citrate-phosphate buffer solution (pH 3.5) and the dispersion was transferred to a quartz cuvette to measure fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence was measured from an excitation wavelength of 355 nm and emission was recorded from 400 to 700 nm. The slit width was 10 nm and the measurement was performed at 25 οC (room temperature).

Milk samples were pretreated as described in a previous report [29]. Briefly, 2.0 mL of milk was weighed in a 10 mL polypropylene tube. To precipitate proteins, 5.0 mL of acetonitrile was added and the tube was vortexed for 20 s. After centrifugation for 15 min at 5000 rpm, the supernatant was separated, evaporated at 60 οC and dissolved in 4.0 mL of deionized water. The pretreated milk samples were then loaded with nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes for extraction and ceftazidime detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ceftazidime by HPLC technique
ce:section.ce:para: The HLPC analysis of ceftazidime was modified from previous work [30]. Ceftazidime was detected using an 1100 series system (Agilent Technologies, Germany). System control and data acquisition were enabled by Chemstation software (Agilent Technologies, Germany). The column was a Fortis™ C18 (5 μm particles size, 150 × 4.6 mm) (Fortis Technologies Ltd., United Kingdom) and the mobile phase consisted of 0.05% formic acid and acetonitrile (88:12, v/v) at a flow rate of 0.8 mL min−1 and column temperature of 40 οC. UV detection wavelength was set at 260 nm and the sample volume was 20 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and nanocomposite fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of GQDs nanoparticles was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The nanoparticles were uniformly spherical and smooth with an average particle size of about 14.5 ± 2.2 nm (Fig. 2 A). The surface morphology of GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes were uniformly spherical with rough surfaces (Fig. 2B) and an average diameter of 360–440 nm (Fig. 2C). The roughness of the surfaces indicated that the specific gravities were generated on the surface of the nanocomposite probes.

The saturation magnetization of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was determined by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The VSM curves of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe (Fig. 2D) showed that saturation magnetization was 0.30 emu g−1: enough to enable rapid isolation of the nanocomposite probe from the solution using an external magnet.

BET specific surface areas of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP and nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/NIP probes were 22.1821 m2g-1 and 16.7400 m2g-1, respectively. The greater surface area of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was due to the specific binding sites of ceftazidime.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometry of GQDs showed that the maximum wavelength adsorbed by the GQDs was 320 nm and they produced a narrow, symmetric fluorescence spectrum around a maximum emission wavelength of about 435 nm (Fig. S1). The fluorescence spectrum of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe in the presence of the ceftazidime showed a low intensity (Fig. S2a) but after the removal of ceftazidime (Fig. S2b), intensity recovered to the same level as emission intensity of the NIP (Fig. S2c). These results indicated that the ceftazidime was completely removed from the magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe, which was then ready for detection of the target analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the synthesis of magnetic nanocomposite probe and detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the magnetic nanocomposite probe and detection conditions were investigated and optimized. The affecting parameters included the ratio of template to functional monomer to cross-linker, incubation time, stirring rate, amount of magnetite nanoparticles, sample volume, and pH of the dispersion solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication strategy of the magnetic nanocomposite probe was optimized by investigating the mole ratios of template (T) to functional monomer (M) and template to cross-linker (C). The ratio of T: M was varied from 1: 3 to 1: 6 to 1: 9 and of T: C from 1:10 to 1:20 to 1:30. The mole ratio of T: M: C of 1:6:20 provided the highest quenching efficiency (Fig. 3 ). Low quenching efficiency was observed at lower amounts of monomer (1:3) due to the low number of functional groups in the nanocomposite probe. Quenching efficiency was also low when the high amount of monomer was used (1:9). This result was caused by inhibition of target analyte-binding and obstruction of recognition sites by excess monomer. The mole ratio of T: C also affected quenching efficiency for the detection of the target analyte. Quenching efficiency was higher at the ratio of 1:20 than 1:10 but decreased with the increment of cross-linker at the ratio of 1:30. The low quenching efficiency obtained at the ratio of 1:10 was due to the weak physical structure produced at this level of MIP. A weaker structure can lead to less effective formation of specific binding sites. However, too high an amount of cross-linker (1:30) also produced low quenching efficiency. Excessive cross-linking can block the movement and diffusion of the functional monomer (APTES), interfering with its binding to template molecules and producing a low number of binding sites within the MIP. Therefore, a molar ratio of T: M: C of 1:6:20 was chosen for the preparation of the magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time and stirring rate
ce:section.ce:para: The appropriate binding time between the magnetic nanocomposite probe and the target analyte was determined by incubating them together for 10, 20 and 30 min under stirring at 350, 500 and 650 rpm. The highest quenching efficiency was obtained at the incubation time of 20 min and stirring rate of 500 rpm (Fig. 4 A). Quenching efficiency increased from 10 min to 20 min and then remained constant to 30 min. The low quenching efficiency obtained at the stirring rate of 300 rpm might have been due to a low diffusion rate of the target analyte toward specific binding cavities of the nanocomposite probe. Quenching efficiency was also low at 650 rpm may be due to the incomplete binding of the target analyte with the recognition sites under high speed stirring. Therefore, the incubation time of 20 min and stirring rate of 500 rpm were chosen for the preconcentration and detection of ceftazidime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of magnetite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of varying the amount of magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2) used to fabricate the magnetic nanocomposite probe was investigated by using 5.0, 10, 25 and 50 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fig. 4B). The results showed that 10 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 provided the best sensitivity for ceftazidime detection. The lower sensitivity obtained at 5 mg was probably due to incomplete recovery of the probes from the sample solution with the magnet. Sensitivity was also low at 25 and 50 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2, possibly because the greater concentration of magnetite nanoparticles inhibited the formation of MIP and, therefore, fewer specific recognition sites were produced for analyte adsorption.

The sample volume was varied to obtain the highest enrichment factor and sensitivity. Therefore, the optimal value reflected a compromise between enrichment and sensitivity. The sample volume of 4.0 mL was selected as the optimal value since it provided the highest enrichment factor with high sensitivity (Fig. 4C). Larger sample volumes provided higher enrichment factors but exhibited lower sensitivity due to lower adsorption rates between the target analyte and specific recognition sites.

The nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was dispersed in citrate-phosphate buffer solutions at pH 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5 and 5.0. The effect on fluorescence quenching was observed. The highest fluorescence quenching was obtained in buffer at pH 3.5 (Fig. 4D). Interaction between the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe and ceftazidime molecules depended on hydrogen bonding. At pH 3.0, fluorescence quenching was low because protonation of the functional monomer APTES reduced interaction with ceftazidime. However, fluorescence quenching was also low at pH higher than 3.5 because silica layers of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were damaged and the specific binding sites of the GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probes were disrupted. Therefore, citrate-phosphate buffer at pH 3.5 was selected as the optimum dispersion pH for further experiments.

The nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe was fabricated in the optimum condition and used with an optosensor under the optimum conditions of detection. The analytical performances of the system were evaluated. The performances investigated included linearity, limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ). The developed optosensor exhibited good linearity between 0.10 and 10.0 μg L−1. The LOD and LOQ were 0.05 and 0.17 μg L−1, respectively, based on 3SD/slope and 10SD/slope. The fluorescence spectra of nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP and GQDs@Fe3O4/NIP probes in the presence of various ceftazidime concentrations showed that the fluorescence intensity of the MIP probe decreased proportionally to ceftazidime concentration, while the intensity of the NIP probe only slightly decreased with increasing ceftazidime concentration (Fig. 5 A and B). Fig. 5C and D show the calibration curve of the developed probe and a photograph of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe under UV light with and without ceftazidime. The good fluorescence quenching of the nanocomposite MIP probe was due to the specific interaction between binding sites on the surface of the probe and ceftazidime. The poor fluorescence quenching of the NIP probe was due to physical, non-specific binding adsorption between the surface of the nanocomposite probes and target analyte.

The quenching of the magnetic nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe is described by the Stern-Volmer equation as follows:
F0/F = 1 +
K sv
[ X ]
,
where F and F0 are the fluorescence signal with and without ceftazidime, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [X] is the ceftazidime concentration. The fluorescence quenching in the presence of ceftazidime was due to the energy transfer from GQDs when ceftazidime bound with amino groups of APTES on the surface of GQDs. The quenching mechanism was not due to energy resonance transfer as the emission spectrum of the magnetic nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe did not overlap with the absorption spectrum of ceftazidime (Fig. S3).
The specificity of the GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was evaluated in terms of imprinting factor (IF), which was calculated by the ratio of Ksv, MIP and Ksv, NIP. In this work, the IF of ceftazidime was 8.40.

To investigate the selectivity of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe toward the detection of ceftazidime, the developed probe was used to detect analogue structures of ceftazidime that included ceftriaxone, cefadroxil, cephalexin and cefazolin. The nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe exhibited high sensitivity toward ceftazidime and much lower sensitivity toward the analogue compounds (Fig. 6 A). The non-imprinted nanocomposite probe exhibited low sensitivity toward both ceftazidime and the structure analogue compounds. The surface of the MIP probe provided many specific binding sites created by template molecules, whereas no specific binding sites were created on the surface of the NIP probe. Competitive binding to the magnetic nanocomposite probes was also studied by examining the quenching efficiency of MIP and NIP nanocomposite probes in the presence of different mole ratios of ceftazidime to ceftriaxone. The quenching efficiency of the nanocomposite MIP probe was not significantly changed when the ratio of ceftazidime to ceftriaxone increased (Fig. 6B). These results implied that the developed optosensor was sufficiently selective toward the ceftazidime template molecule.

The stability of the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was investigated by measuring emission intensity every 30 min. The emission intensity of the magnetic nanocomposite probe did not significantly change within 8 h, producing more than 95% of the original intensity (Fig. S4A). This result confirmed the good stability of the synthesized magnetic nanocomposite probe. The MIP layer can help to protect the quantum dots.

The reproducibility of the magnetic nanocomposite probe was investigated by preparing six different lots in the optimal condition. The RSDs of average sensitivity was 1.6% which indicated the good reproducibility of the fabrication of the probe (Fig. S4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of optosensor using nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe with other works
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances of the optosensor using GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe for ceftazidime detection were compared with performances obtained from previous works (Table 2 ). The developed optosensor provided a wide linear range and LOD was better than in other methods. Recovery and precision were better than or comparable to other works. The comparison implied that the optosensor using the nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP probe was highly sensitive for the detection of ceftazidime. Moreover, analysis with the optosensor was more economical both in time and cost than analysis with the chromatographic method. Therefore, this optosensor offers an alternative, highly sensitive, selective and accurate method for trace ceftazidime detection.

A sensing system using a nanocomposite GQDs@Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe and an optical sensor was developed and utilized for ceftazidime detection. Integration of GQDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and MIP, the optosensor was a highly sensitive and selective tool for the analysis of ceftazidime at trace level. The developed optosensor detected ceftazidime in milk with an accuracy (recoveries of 90.7–99.2%) comparable to that of an HPLC technique. The developed optosensor is easy to operate, the procedure is rapid and the cost of equipment low. This developed system can be modified for the preconcentration and detection of other target analytes in various sample matrices.

Opas Bunkoed: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Phannika Raksawong: Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft. Ratchada Chaowana: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Piyaluk Nurerk: Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Prince of Songkla University (SCI6202115N-0), Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), National Research Council of Thailand, Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. Ratchada Chaowana was supported by the Faculty of Science Research Fund, Prince of Songkla University, Thailand, Contract No. 1-2561-02-004. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading. This research was also supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship of Prince of Songkla University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Piperacillin-tazobactam should be preferred to third-generation cephalosporins to treat wild-type inducible AmpC-producing Enterobacterales in critically ill patients with hospital or ventilator-acquired pneumonia
J. Crit. Care

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Carrie, G. Bardonneau, L. Petit, A. Ouattara, D. Gruson, B. Pereira, M. Crit. Care 56 (2020) 6-11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcrc.2019.11.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a new electrochemical sensor based on molecular imprinting recognition sites on multiwall carbon nanotube surface for analysis of ceftazidime in real samples
Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Torkashvand, M.B. Gholivand, G. Malekzadeh, Construction of a new electrochemical sensor based on molecular imprinting recognition sites on multiwall carbon nanotube surface for analysis of ceftazidime in real samples, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 231 (2016) 759-767. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.03.061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical investigation and determination of ceftazidime in pharmaceutical dosage forms and human urine
J. Anal. Chem.
